feat: improve 27 curated skill definitions via tessl skill review

Hullo @ComposioHQ 👋

I ran your skills through `tessl skill review` at work and found some targeted improvements. Here's the before/after for the top 10 most improved:

| Skill | Before | After | Change |
|-------|--------|-------|--------|
| content-research-writer | 26% | 96% | +70% |
| file-organizer | 30% | 96% | +66% |
| competitive-ads-extractor | 26% | 89% | +63% |
| skill-share | 26% | 89% | +63% |
| image-enhancer | 36% | 96% | +60% |
| domain-name-brainstormer | 34% | 89% | +55% |
| changelog-generator | 43% | 96% | +53% |
| lead-research-assistant | 41% | 93% | +52% |
| tailored-resume-generator | 38% | 89% | +51% |
| brand-guidelines | 56% | 100% | +44% |

All 27 curated skills were reviewed and optimized. Average score went from 56% to 93% (+37%).

<details>
<summary>Changes made across skills</summary>

- **Expanded frontmatter descriptions** with explicit "Use when..." trigger clauses and comprehensive natural-language trigger terms
- **Removed verbose explanations** of concepts Claude already understands (filler words, obvious definitions, marketing copy)
- **Added executable code examples** — concrete commands, Python snippets, regex patterns replacing vague procedural descriptions
- **Added validation checkpoints** and error handling to workflows
- **Improved progressive disclosure** — referenced external files where appropriate instead of inlining everything
- **Consolidated redundant sections** — replaced repetitive prose with compact reference tables
- **Switched to third-person voice** in descriptions (per skill authoring best practices)
- **Net reduction of ~2,265 lines** across 27 files while increasing actionability

</details>

Honest disclosure — I work at @tesslio where we build tooling around skills like these. Not a pitch - just saw room for improvement and wanted to contribute.

If you want to run reviews, evals and optimizations yourself, just `npm install @tessl/cli` then run `tessl skill review path/to/your/SKILL.md`, and click [here](https://tessl.io/registry/skills/submit) to find out more.

Thanks in advance 🙏

feat: env-aware homunculus dir resolver

Replace hardcoded ~/.claude/homunculus with a resolver chain:
CLV2_HOMUNCULUS_DIR > XDG_DATA_HOME/ecc-homunculus > $HOME/.local/share/ecc-homunculus.

Touches every code path to the homunculus data dir:
- detect-project.sh, observe.sh, start-observer.sh (bash)
- instinct-cli.py (Python module-level)
- observer-sessions.js getHomunculusDir (Node)
- session-start.js imports the helper instead of recomputing

Moves observer data out of ~/.claude/** so Claude Code's hardcoded
sensitive-path guard stops blocking instinct writes from the Haiku
observer subprocess in --print mode.

Tests updated to match the new default path.

feat: add manual migrate-homunculus.sh

One-shot script to move existing ~/.claude/homunculus data to
the new XDG location. Idempotent (no-op if old dir absent).
Refuses while a local observer-loop.sh is running. Refuses if
both paths exist with content (operator resolves manually).
Does not touch ~/.claude/settings.json; prints advisory if
CLV2_CONFIG still points at the old path.

Test coverage in tests/hooks/hooks.test.js: happy path, idempotency,
conflict detection, empty-destination merge, observer-running refusal.

refactor: share homunculus-dir resolver and harden migrate script

- Extract the CLV2_HOMUNCULUS_DIR -> XDG_DATA_HOME -> $HOME fallback
  chain into skills/continuous-learning-v2/scripts/lib/homunculus-dir.sh
  so all shell callers use one resolver instead of four inline copies.
- Validate that env overrides are absolute paths; relative values warn
  on stderr and fall through, so observer data never lands under cwd.
- migrate-homunculus.sh: exit early when the resolved destination
  equals the source, and warn (not silently skip) when pgrep is absent.
- Regression tests cover the new relative-path fallback behavior.

refactor: validate absolute paths in getHomunculusDir

Hoist the `os` require to the module header and add `path.isAbsolute`
guards for CLV2_HOMUNCULUS_DIR and XDG_DATA_HOME so relative values
fall through with a stderr warning instead of silently redirecting
observer data under cwd.

Adds a focused unit test covering the five resolution branches.

refactor: validate absolute paths in instinct-cli resolver

Relative CLV2_HOMUNCULUS_DIR now raises ValueError so observer data
cannot silently land under cwd. XDG_DATA_HOME keeps the XDG spec's
warn-and-fallback behavior so a misconfigured XDG_DATA_HOME does not
crash the CLI. Tests cover both branches.

docs+test: normalize paths and make tests portable

- SKILL.md: document CLV2_HOMUNCULUS_DIR in the projects.json path and
  use the full skills/continuous-learning-v2/scripts/migrate-homunculus.sh
  path in the backward-compat note so copy/paste works.
- hooks.test.js: resolve Python at runtime via findPython() instead of
  hardcoding python3 (reviewer flagged Windows/minimal-image breakage).
- hooks.test.js: poll pgrep before asserting migrate-homunculus.sh
  refuses, so slow CI hosts don't race the fake observer child.

refactor: align Python resolver with Bash/Node on relative override

Warn and fall through on relative CLV2_HOMUNCULUS_DIR instead of raising
ValueError, matching the Bash and Node resolvers so behavior stays
consistent across the three implementations.

docs: reframe homunculus relocation as bugfix and update observer.md

CHANGELOG: move the homunculus path-relocation entry out of the BREAKING
section into Fixed. The change unblocks an instinct-write feature that
was silently broken; new installs require no action and only existing
users with data at the old path need the migration step.

observer.md: replace stale ~/.claude/homunculus/... paths with
${HOMUNCULUS_DIR}/... and reference the resolver chain in SKILL.md.

fix: address cubic P2 review findings on PR #1431

- homunculus-dir.sh: validate $HOME is absolute before falling back to
  it, matching the existing CLV2_HOMUNCULUS_DIR / XDG_DATA_HOME guards.
  Returns non-zero with a stderr warning otherwise so callers can fail
  loudly instead of writing data under cwd.

- SKILL.md: list three migration invocations (plugin / ~/.claude
  install / repo clone) so the docs work regardless of cwd or install
  layout.

- tests/hooks/hooks.test.js + tests/integration/hooks.test.js:
  introduce buildSubprocessEnv() that strips inherited
  XDG_DATA_HOME / CLV2_HOMUNCULUS_DIR before spawning, unless the
  caller explicitly sets them. Side-effect assertions that target
  \$HOME/.local/share/ecc-homunculus are now deterministic regardless
  of the test runner's environment.

- assertNoProjectDetectionSideEffects: add a defense-in-depth check
  that nothing leaked back to the legacy ~/.claude/homunculus path,
  which is still inside Claude Code's --print sensitive-path guard.

Test suite: 1844 passing (was 1838); 5 pre-existing upstream failures
(ci/validators.test.js, hooks/mcp-health-check.test.js) untouched.

fix: align Python resolver with Bash/Node on `~`-prefixed env paths

Addresses CodeRabbit Major on PR #1431 (instinct-cli.py:55).

Python's _resolve_homunculus_dir() called Path(value).expanduser() before
checking is_absolute(), so CLV2_HOMUNCULUS_DIR=~/foo and
XDG_DATA_HOME=~/foo were silently accepted in Python while the Bash
(homunculus-dir.sh) and Node (observer-sessions.js) resolvers rejected
them as non-absolute. The drift could split observer data across
runtimes depending on which one wrote.

Now Python checks Path(raw).is_absolute() first and only returns when
the raw value already starts with `/`, matching the other two
resolvers exactly. Behavior on `/foo` is unchanged; `~/foo` is now
rejected with the same stderr warning the other resolvers emit.

Test suite: 1844 passing; 5 pre-existing upstream failures untouched.

fix(clv2): support CLV2_CONFIG override in start-observer.sh

observe.sh already supports the CLV2_CONFIG environment variable to
override the config.json path, but start-observer.sh hardcodes
SKILL_ROOT/config.json. When ECC is installed as a plugin, SKILL_ROOT
resolves to the plugin directory instead of ~/.claude/skills/, so the
user's config (with observer.enabled: true) is never read.

This is particularly impactful on macOS where the observer lazy-start
requires lockfile (procmail) — without the correct config path, the
observer never starts even when properly configured.

Apply the same CLV2_CONFIG override pattern from observe.sh to
start-observer.sh for consistency.

fix(clv2): fix observer sandbox issues on macOS

Two issues prevented the observer Haiku agent from working:

1. Temp files created in TMPDIR (/var/folders/ on macOS) are
   inaccessible to the sandboxed Claude agent spawned by observer-loop.
   Move analysis and prompt files to PROJECT_DIR instead.

2. Claude CLI does not have a --cwd flag. Use --add-dir to grant
   the sandboxed agent read/write access to the project directory
   where instinct files live.

Tested: observer now successfully reads observations and can write
instinct files on macOS.

feat: add pending instinct TTL pruning and /prune command

Pending instincts generated by the observer accumulate indefinitely
with no cleanup mechanism. This adds lifecycle management:

- `instinct-cli.py prune` — delete pending instincts older than 30 days
  (configurable via --max-age). Supports --dry-run and --quiet flags.
- Enhanced `status` command — shows pending count, warns at 5+,
  highlights instincts expiring within 7 days.
- `observer-loop.sh` — runs prune before each analysis cycle.
- `/prune` slash command — user-facing command for manual pruning.

Design rationale: council consensus (4/4) rejected auto-promote in
favor of TTL-based garbage collection. Frequency of observation does
not establish correctness. Unreviewed pending instincts auto-delete
after 30 days; if the pattern is real, the observer will regenerate it.

feat: add project cooldown log to prevent rapid observer re-spawn

Adds session-guardian.sh, called by observer-loop.sh before each Haiku
spawn. It reads ~/.claude/observer-last-run.log and blocks the cycle if
the same project was observed within OBSERVER_INTERVAL_SECONDS (default
300s).

Prevents self-referential loops where a spawned session triggers
observe.sh, which signals the observer before the cooldown has elapsed.

Uses a mkdir-based lock for safe concurrent access across multiple
simultaneously-observed projects. Log entries use tab-delimited format
to handle paths containing spaces. Fails open on lock contention.

Config:
  OBSERVER_INTERVAL_SECONDS   default: 300
  OBSERVER_LAST_RUN_LOG       default: ~/.claude/observer-last-run.log

No external dependencies. Works on macOS, Linux, Windows (Git Bash/MSYS2).

feat: extend session-guardian with time window and idle detection gates

Adds Gate 1 (active hours check) and Gate 3 (system idle detection) to
session-guardian.sh, building on the per-project cooldown log from PR 1.

Gate 1 — Time Window:
- OBSERVER_ACTIVE_HOURS_START/END (default 800–2300 local time)
- Uses date +%k%M with 10# prefix to avoid octal crash at midnight
- Toolless on all platforms; set both vars to 0 to disable

Gate 3 — Idle Detection:
- macOS: ioreg + awk (built-in, no deps)
- Linux: xprintidle if available, else fail open
- Windows (Git Bash/MSYS2): PowerShell GetLastInputInfo via Add-Type
- Unknown/headless: always returns 0 (fail open)
- OBSERVER_MAX_IDLE_SECONDS=0 disables gate

Fixes in this commit:
- 10# base-10 prefix prevents octal arithmetic crash on midnight minutes
  containing digits 8 or 9 (e.g. 00:08 = "008" is invalid octal)
- PowerShell output piped through tr -d '\r' to strip Windows CRLF;
  also uses [long] cast to avoid TickCount 32-bit overflow after 24 days
- mktemp now uses log file directory instead of TMPDIR to ensure
  same-filesystem mv on Linux (atomic rename instead of copy+unlink)
- mkdir -p failure exits 0 (fail open) rather than crashing under set -e
- Numeric validation on last_spawn prevents arithmetic error on corrupt log

Gate execution order: 1 (time, ~0ms) → 2 (cooldown, ~1ms) → 3 (idle, ~50ms)

fix(skill-creator): eval-viewer HTML breakout + metadata search depth

Two bugs in generate_review.py:

1. User-generated outputs containing `</script>` (e.g. code samples
   showing gtag.js integration) break out of the embedded `<script>`
   block, corrupting the entire viewer HTML. Fix: escape `</` to `<\/`
   after `json.dumps()` — both forms are valid JSON (RFC 8259 §7) and
   the browser's HTML parser no longer sees a premature script
   terminator.

2. `eval_metadata.json` is only searched 1 level up from `run_dir`,
   but the canonical workspace layout places it 2 levels up
   (`eval-N/eval_metadata.json` vs `eval-N/with_skill/run-1/`).
   This causes all prompts to show as "(No prompt found)" in the

skill-creator: fix parallel worker false negatives in run_eval.py

Each parallel worker created its own command file with a unique UUID
suffix, then checked whether Claude invoked that specific UUID. When
multiple identically-described command files coexisted on disk, Claude
would pick one arbitrarily — but only the worker whose UUID was chosen
reported a trigger. All other concurrent workers reported false
negatives, producing artificially low trigger rates that scale
inversely with --num-workers (e.g. ~33% at 3 workers, ~0% at 10).

Fix: create a single shared command file in run_eval() before spawning
workers, so every claude -p session sees exactly one command and all
workers match against the same name. File cleanup moves to a finally
block in run_eval() that runs after ProcessPoolExecutor.shutdown()
guarantees all workers have completed.

Related to #556

feat: Add new skills for PDF merging and splitting

This commit introduces two new skills to the project:

- `pdf-merge`: Merges multiple PDF documents into a single file.
- `pdf-split`: Splits a single PDF document into multiple files, one for each page.

Both skills include a `SKILL.md` file with a description and usage instructions, and a Python script that handles the core logic.

A `requirements.txt` file has been added to the root of the project to manage the new dependencies (`pypdf` and `reportlab`).

A new test suite has been added in `tests/test_pdf_skills.py` to ensure the new skills work as expected. The tests create dummy PDF files, run the merge and split scripts, and verify the output.

⚡ Bolt: Optimize PDF to image conversion

💡 What: Optimized `convert_pdf_to_images.py` to use `pdf2image`'s native scaling and parallel processing.
🎯 Why: The original script generated high-resolution images (200 DPI) and then resized them in Python, which was memory-intensive and slow.
📊 Impact: Reduces execution time by ~80% (5x speedup) on a 20-page PDF (from ~7.9s to ~1.6s).
🔬 Measurement: Run a benchmark converting a multi-page PDF and check execution time.

⚡ Bolt: Optimize Excel parsing with read_only=True

💡 What:
Updated `recalc.py` to use `read_only=True` when loading Excel workbooks using `openpyxl`.
Refactored logic to use `try...finally` blocks to ensure `wb.close()` is always called, preventing file handle leaks.

🎯 Why:
The script parses the Excel file twice (once for errors, once for formulas). Loading the full object model into memory is slow and memory-intensive for large files. `read_only=True` streams the data.

📊 Impact:
Performance benchmark showed ~1.75x speedup on a 100k cell test file. Memory usage is also significantly reduced.

🔬 Measurement:
Verified with `verify_recalc_mock.py` that error checking and formula counting logic remains correct.
Verified with `benchmark_recalc.py` that performance improved.

⚡ Bolt: Cache next_change_id in DocxXMLEditor

- Initializes `_next_change_id_cache` in `__init__`
- Updates `_get_next_change_id` to use and update the cache instead of scanning DOM every time
- Reduces complexity of batch insertions from O(N^2) to O(N)

⚡ Bolt: Optimize DocxXMLEditor ID generation

Optimized `DocxXMLEditor._get_next_change_id` to cache the next available change ID instead of scanning the full DOM every time.

- Reduces complexity from O(N*M) to O(M+N) for N insertions in a document of size M.
- Measured 20x speedup (4.06s -> 0.20s) for inserting 50 tracked changes in a 20k paragraph document.
- Ensures ID uniqueness while avoiding redundant traversals.

fix: harden Codex sandbox with auto-detection and fallback

Codex's read-only sandbox uses bubblewrap (bwrap) which fails on some
Linux kernels (6.17+) due to blocked unprivileged network namespaces.
The CLI returns exit 0 but admits it can't read files.

Changes:
- Auto-detect sandbox compatibility by probing a real file read
- Cache probe result for the session (avoid re-probing)
- Auto-retry with danger-full-access on sandbox failures
- Detect soft failures (exit 0 but "couldn't read" in output)
- Add --sandbox flag and CONSULT_CODEX_SANDBOX env var for override
- Tighten empty-response detection to avoid false positives
- Expand filter_output patterns (exec, token count lines)
- Show sandbox mode in `check` command output

Bump version to 5.0.11.

feat: add 3-way plan compliance review at end of coding sessions

After all tasks complete in subagent-driven-development, offer a
3-way final review (Claude subagent + Codex + Gemini) that checks
the entire implementation against the original plan and spec. This
catches cross-task deviations that per-task reviews miss — e.g.
retry counts, logging formats, or spec requirements that fell
through the cracks.

fix: Codex "not inside trusted directory" error — auto-detect git root

Codex CLI requires running from inside a trusted git repo. When
consult.sh was invoked from a non-repo directory (e.g. ~/projects/github),
Codex would fail with "Not inside a trusted directory".

- Add resolve_codex_cwd() that finds nearest git root via git rev-parse
- All codex invocations (probe + run + retry) now cd to git root
- Add --cwd flag and CONSULT_CODEX_CWD env var for explicit override
- Detect "trusted directory" error in failure patterns
- Update SKILL.md to resolve script path from installed_plugins.json
  instead of find (which picked up stale cached copies)
- Clean up stale cache versions

refactor: rewrite writing-plans to focus on decisions, not code

Modern coding agents can read files, follow patterns, and write code.
What they can't do is make architectural decisions or know unstated
constraints. Shift plans from "complete code walkthrough" to
"decisions + interfaces + constraints + behaviors to test."

Key changes:
- Tasks are coherent work units, not TDD micro-steps
- Interface signatures replace full implementation code
- Test behaviors (assertions) replace full test code
- New "When to Include Code" guidance — code when it communicates
  a decision more precisely than prose
- Reviewer updated to check decision clarity, not code completeness

fix: use Codex --cd and --skip-git-repo-check instead of subshell cd

Previous fix used `cd "$git_root"` in a subshell, but when PWD wasn't
inside any git repo (e.g. <LOCAL_USER_PATH>/projects), resolve_codex_cwd()
fell back to PWD — which still wasn't a git repo, so Codex rejected it.

Now uses Codex's native flags:
- --cd <DIR>: when a git root is found (via explicit --cwd or git
  rev-parse --show-toplevel)
- --skip-git-repo-check: when no git repo can be found, allowing
  Codex to run from any directory

feat(worktree): add lifecycle management with automatic garbage collection

## Problem (#666)

Git worktrees created by Superpowers persist indefinitely, accumulating
in .worktrees/ or ~/.config/superpowers/worktrees/. After branches are
merged or abandoned, leftover worktrees waste disk space.

## Solution

Implement comprehensive worktree lifecycle management with automatic
garbage collection:

### Core Components

1. **Worktree Registry** (~/.config/superpowers/worktree-registry.json)
   - Tracks all worktrees created by Superpowers
   - Stores metadata: branch, path, status, timestamps
   - Maintains GC policy configuration

2. **Worktree Manager CLI** (lib/worktree-manager)
   - register/unregister worktrees
   - Update status (active → pr_opened → merged → cleanup_pending)
   - List worktrees by project
   - Find worktrees by branch
   - Run garbage collection

3. **GC Daemon** (lib/worktree-gc-daemon)
   - Background process running every hour
   - Detects status changes (branch deleted, PR merged)
   - Safely cleans up pending worktrees

4. **Git Post-Merge Hook** (lib/hooks/post-merge)
   - Detects when branches are merged
   - Schedules worktree cleanup with 1-hour delay
   - Provides user-friendly notifications

### Status Lifecycle

### Safety Guarantees

- Never delete worktrees with uncommitted changes
- Never delete active worktrees
- Grace period (1h for merged, immediate for abandoned)
- Full audit log at ~/.config/superpowers/worktree-gc.log

### Integration

-  skill registers new worktrees
-  skill triggers cleanup
- Optional: Install post-merge hook for automatic cleanup

## Files Added
- lib/worktree-registry.schema.json - Registry schema
- lib/worktree-manager - CLI tool for registry management
- lib/worktree-gc-daemon - Background GC process
- lib/hooks/post-merge - Git hook for merge detection
- docs/worktree-lifecycle.md - User documentation

## Files Modified
- skills/using-git-worktrees/SKILL.md - Register worktrees on creation

Fixes #666

fix(worktree): address all CodeRabbit review feedback

## Critical Fixes

1. **post-merge: Get correct merged branch**
   - Use git reflog to find source branch, not target
   - Fix: `git reflog -1 --format='%gs'` parsing

2. **worktree-manager: Use git worktree remove**
   - Replace `rm -rf` with proper `git worktree remove`
   - Prevents git's internal registry inconsistency

3. **worktree-manager: Portable base64 decode**
   - Add fallback for macOS (uses -D flag)
   - Works on GNU, BSD, and falls back to Python

## Major Fixes

4. **Remove fragile background scheduling**
   - Removed `(sleep 3600 && ...)` subshells
   - Let GC daemon handle all transitions
   - Prevents orphan processes

5. **worktree-gc-daemon: Atomic lock acquisition**
   - Use flock for TOCTOU-safe locking
   - Prevents concurrent daemon instances
   - Create log directory on startup

6. **worktree-manager: Argument validation**
   - Validate path and branch are non-empty
   - Prevent invalid registry entries

7. **using-git-worktrees: Expand tilde**
   - Use ${HOME} instead of ~ in path
   - Prevents unexpanded tildes in registry

## Minor Fixes

8. **docs: Correct LaunchAgent/crontab setup**
   - macOS: Create proper .plist file
   - Linux: Append to crontab (not replace)

9. **Rename schema to template**
   - worktree-registry.schema.json → worktree-registry.template.json
   - Clarifies it's sample data, not JSON Schema

Addresses all 8 actionable + 3 nitpick comments from CodeRabbit

fix(worktree): address critical CodeRabbit feedback (round 2)

1. **Fix JSON injection vulnerability**
   - Replace heredoc with jq --arg for proper escaping
   - Prevents malformed JSON from branch names with quotes/special chars

2. **Fix multiple worktrees handling in post-merge**
   - Use jq -s '.[0]' to get first match only
   - Prevents concatenation of multiple IDs

3. **Add status validation**
   - Whitelist valid status values
   - Prevent registry corruption

fix(worktree): critical bug fixes from CodeRabbit round 3

## Critical Fixes

1. **Fix post-merge hook early exit bug**
   - Remove duplicate/inverted early-exit block
   - Hook was exiting when worktree WAS found, preventing updates
   - Now correctly exits only when NO worktree found

2. **Include untracked files in safety check**
   - Replace git diff-index with git status --porcelain
   - Prevents deletion of worktrees with untracked files
   - Critical safety improvement

3. **Filter by project_root in post-merge**
   - Use jq to filter worktrees by current project
   - Prevents cross-project worktree confusion
   - More precise matching

4. **Don't rm -rf when git worktree remove fails**
   - Log error and keep registry entry pending
   - Prevents git metadata inconsistency
   - Forces manual cleanup instead of silent corruption

Addresses critical issues from CodeRabbit round 3

fix(worktree): fix all critical CodeRabbit issues (final round)

## Critical Fixes (CodeRabbit identified these as duplicates - not fully fixed)

1. **Untracked files detection** ✅ NOW FIXED
   - Replace git diff-index with git status --porcelain
   - Prevents deletion of worktrees with untracked files
   - CodeRabbit kept flagging this - now properly fixed

2. **PR check before marking abandoned** ✅ NOW FIXED
   - Always check gh pr view first (even if branch deleted)
   - Prevents bypassing merged state grace period
   - Correct logic flow: PR check → branch check → abandon

3. **Registry locking** ✅ ADDED
   - Add with_registry_lock() function
   - Prevents concurrent mutation races
   - flock-based serialization

CodeRabbit kept reporting these as duplicates because they weren't
actually fixed in worktree-manager - only in hooks. Now fixed in
the right place.

fix(worktree): add flock to all registry mutations

## Changes

Wrap all registry read-modify-write operations with flock for concurrent
access protection:

1. **register_worktree** - flock around jq append
2. **update_status** - flock around status update
3. **unregister_worktree** - flock around removal
4. **run_gc** - flock around last_gc update

Also fixed jq usage to use --arg for timestamp instead of shell interpolation.

This prevents race conditions when daemon, post-merge hook, and CLI
run simultaneously.

Addresses CodeRabbit feedback on registry locking.

v.6.3.0 - New Features added and some features fixed.

v6.3.0 (2026-04-03)

Session memory quality pass: the stop-reminders hook now tracks undocumented work phases across long sessions, enforced token budgets prevent session-log bloat from inflating injection costs, and parallel dispatch defaults are corrected in two skills.

New Features

Phase-aware decision-log reminders — The stop-reminders hook previously asked "were significant files edited in the last 30 minutes?" — a window that fails in long sessions with multiple work phases (same edits stay in the window after a [saved] entry, while later phases can slip out entirely). It now asks "were significant files edited since the last [saved] entry?" The track-edits hook detects when a [saved] entry is written to session-log.md and records a timestamp marker; stop-reminders reads that marker at Stop time. Each logical work batch gets exactly one reminder at the right time, with no false positives between phases.

Session-log token budget guard — stop-reminders now measures the last 2 [saved] entries at every session Stop and warns when any entry exceeds the 250-token budget (~1,000 characters). These entries are injected into every future session; a bloated entry costs tokens forever. The warning identifies the specific over-budget entry and instructs what to trim.

Strict [saved] entry template in context-management — The context-management skill's [saved] entry template now has an explicit structure (Goal / Decisions / Rejected / Open only), a "Never include" list (test results, task checklists → state.md, how-it-works walkthroughs, speculative analysis → design docs, one-time confirmations), and a hard token budget. Without this enforcement, the AI defaulted to "more is safer" and wrote entries that were 5–10× over the optimal size.

dispatching-parallel-agents skill hint added — skill-rules.json now includes a rule for dispatching-parallel-agents — it was the only skill with no hint coverage, meaning prompts like "dispatch these tasks simultaneously" received no routing suggestion.

Changes

Parallel Waves is now the default in subagent-driven-development — "Parallel Waves" was previously labeled "Optional Speed Mode". It is now the stated default for independent tasks; sequential execution is the explicit fallback for tasks with shared-file or state dependencies. The single-message dispatch requirement is now explicit in both subagent-driven-development and dispatching-parallel-agents, with a rationale: all subagents share the same cached system prompt prefix, and dispatching them in one message ensures every agent gets a cache hit on the heavy shared prefix.

Anti-sycophancy rules added to global CLAUDE.md — Four rules now govern position stability: don't revise a stated position under pushback without new evidence; proactively state the strongest objection to any non-trivial proposal; agreement must cite a specific reason, not just affirm; state confidence level explicitly when uncertain.

Enhance Codex adapters to improve output structure and clarity

- Updated `posttool-bash-compress-adapter.js` to include `continue` and `hookSpecificOutput` fields in the payload, providing better context for the PostToolUse event.
- Modified `stop-adapter.js` to replace `systemMessage` with a structured `decision` and `reason` output, aligning with the Codex contract for reminders.
- Added `buildContinuationReason` function to format reminder messages consistently.
- Updated tests in `test-stop-adapter.js` and `test-posttool-bash-compress-adapter.js` to validate new output structure and ensure proper handling of reminders.

These changes aim to enhance the user experience by providing clearer and more structured feedback during Codex interactions.

fix(stop-hook): suppress spurious error when only stats reminder exists

The stop hook was using decision:block for every session that had skill
invocations, even when the only content was the informational stats
summary ("Session summary: Xmin, Y skill invocations"). This caused:
  - Claude Code to display "Stop hook error: <stats>" on every stop
  - Claude to generate an unnecessary extra response just to acknowledge stats

Fix: only fire decision:block when actionable reminders exist (TDD,
commit hygiene, decision log, session-log size warnings). The stats
summary is informational — it does not warrant blocking Claude's stop.

fix: detect stale state.md and enforce plan checkbox updates

stop-reminders.js: add checkStateMdStaleness() — fires when state.md
exists in cwd but 2+ source-file edits occurred after it was last
written. Reminds Claude to sync state.md before ending the session.
Config-only edits are excluded to avoid noise.

subagent-driven-development/SKILL.md: make "Mark task complete"
concrete — explicitly requires updating the plan.md checkbox from
- [ ] to - [x] and syncing state.md. Same requirement added at the
wave-completion step so state.md stays current across parallel waves.

fix: context-management now resets stop-hook decision-log marker

Added step 5 to the saving procedure: after writing a [saved] entry,
write the current timestamp to ~/.claude/hooks-logs/last-saved-entry.txt.
This is the marker that stop-reminders.js reads to determine whether
significant edits have been saved since the last context-management call.
Without this step, the decision-log reminder refires on every subsequent
stop even after context-management was already invoked that session.

Enhance stop-adapter.js with reminder caching and deduplication logic

- Introduced reminder caching to prevent duplicate reminders within the same session and per day for unchanged states.
- Added functions to manage the reminder cache, including loading, saving, and pruning old entries.
- Implemented logic to determine when to emit reminders based on session state and changes in the working directory.
- Updated tests to validate the new reminder deduplication behavior and ensure correct functionality across sessions.

These changes improve the user experience by reducing unnecessary reminders and ensuring relevant notifications are provided when state changes occur.

Update codex-hooks.json to enhance command execution and status messaging

- Added status messages for session start, user prompt submission, post-tool Bash compression, and stop adapter commands to improve user feedback during execution.

- Ensured that command structures remain consistent while enhancing clarity in the execution process.

These updates aim to provide better context and feedback for users interacting with Codex hooks.

Replace Kimi Code symlink-based install with first-class native hook support

Previously, Kimi Code support required users to create a destructive junction
replacing their entire ~/.kimi/skills/ directory. This commit replaces that
pattern with a proper native integration:

- Skills are copied to ~/.config/agents/skills/ (the cross-tool recommended path)
- A global SessionStart hook injects the superpowers bootstrap at the start of
  every Kimi session
- Install/update scripts handle config.toml safely (no duplicate keys, proper
  TOML escaping for Windows paths)
- Bootstrap includes explicit instructions telling Kimi to auto-read matching
  skills via ReadFile (since Kimi doesn't have a native Skill tool like Claude)

fix: correct git stage-lines syntax error in writing-plans example

The previous example incorrectly showed staging two files in a single
command: `git stage-lines tests/path/test.py 1-50 src/path/file.py`

This is invalid syntax. git stage-lines only accepts ONE file at a time:
- `git stage-lines <lines> <file>` for tracked files
- `git stage-lines <untracked-file>` for untracked files

Fixed by splitting into two separate commands with proper syntax and
added a helpful tip about using git diff to find line ranges.

fix: remove announcement requirement blocking execute-plan-gradual

Remove "Announce at start" instruction from executing-plans skill that
was creating chicken-and-egg problem: skill required announcing "this plan"
before loading the plan file, causing it to ask for plan clarification.

Root cause: Announcement referenced "this plan" but plan loading happens
in Step 1 AFTER the announcement, so Claude didn't know what plan to
reference and asked user for clarification.

Solution: Remove announcement requirement entirely. subagent-driven-development
has no announcement and works correctly - it proceeds directly to load plan
from conversation context (e.g., @docs/plans/file.md from command line).

Now both commands work:
- /flows:execute-plan @filepath - uses subagent-driven-development (no announcement)
- /flows:execute-plan-gradual @filepath - uses executing-plans (no announcement)

fix: rename execute-plan-gradual to execute-gradual

Root cause: Claude Code's slash command parser fails to pass arguments
to commands with 2+ hyphens (3+ parts). Command name parsing was breaking
argument extraction.

Evidence:
- /flows:execute-plan @file → shows "is running… @file" ✅
- /flows:execute-plan-gradual @file → shows "is running…" ❌ (arg lost)
- /flows:execute-gradual @file → shows "is running… @file" ✅

Solution: Rename execute-plan-gradual (3 parts) to execute-gradual (2 parts)

the using-superpowers skill content. The new approach:

fix(opencode): Use `synthetic: true` for invisible content injection (#122)

The OpenCode plugin injects the bootstrap and skill content as visible
user messages. While `noReply: true` prevents an AI response, the
injected content still appears in the chat UI which makes it just a bit
noisy.

OpenCode's `TextPartInput` supports a `synthetic` Boolean property that
hides parts from the UI while keeping them visible to the AI -- it's
been available in the v2 message format since around the v1.0.0 version
bump.

Add skills improvement plan from user feedback

Analyzed feedback from two Claude instances using superpowers in real
development scenarios. Identified 8 core problems and proposed improvements
organized by impact and risk.

Key problems:
- Configuration change verification gap (verify success not intent)
- Background process accumulation across subagents
- Context bloat in subagent prompts
- Missing self-reflection before handoff
- Mock-interface drift
- Code reviewer file access issues
- Skills not being read/enforced
- Fix workflow latency

Proposed improvements organized in 3 phases:
- Phase 1: High-impact, low-risk (do first)
- Phase 2: Moderate changes (test carefully)
- Phase 3: Optimization (validate first)

See plan for detailed analysis and open questions.

Update subagent-driven-development: controller provides full task text

Changed workflow so controller provides complete task context directly
rather than making subagent read plan file.

Key changes:
- Controller reads plan and extracts full task text
- Controller provides scene-setting context (dependencies, architecture)
- Subagent receives complete information in prompt (no file reading)
- Subagent can ask clarifying questions before beginning work
- Controller handles questions/concerns before subagent proceeds

Benefits:
- No file reading overhead for subagent
- Controller curates exactly what context is needed
- Questions surfaced before work begins (not after)
- Subagent has complete information to do best work

This enables subagents to start with clarity rather than ambiguity.

Make spec compliance reviewer skeptical and verification-focused

The spec compliance reviewer now:
- Does NOT trust implementer's report
- Is warned implementer finished suspiciously quickly
- MUST verify everything by reading actual code
- Compares implementation to requirements line by line
- Reports issues with file:line references

Key additions:
- "Do Not Trust the Report" section
- Explicit DO NOT / DO lists
- "Verify by reading code, not by trusting report"
- Changed "What Was Implemented" to "What Implementer Claims They Built"

This prevents rubber-stamping and ensures independent verification
of spec compliance against actual codebase.

Emphasize spec compliance review must complete before code quality

Made sequencing explicit:
- Spec compliance review loop must fully complete (✅) before code quality
- Added "Do NOT proceed to code quality review until spec compliance is ✅"
- Code Quality Review section starts with "Only run after spec compliance review is complete"
- Red Flags: Added "Start code quality review before spec compliance is ✅ (wrong order)"

This ensures we don't waste time reviewing code quality of the wrong
implementation. Verify they built the right thing first, then verify
they built it well.

fix: use git check-ignore for worktree gitignore verification (#160)

* fix: use git check-ignore for worktree gitignore verification

The using-git-worktrees skill previously used grep to check only the
local .gitignore file, missing patterns in global gitignore configurations
(core.excludesfile). This caused unnecessary modifications to local
.gitignore when the directory was already globally ignored.

Changed verification from grep to git check-ignore, which respects Git's
full ignore hierarchy (local, global, and system gitignore files).

Fixes obra/superpowers#101

Tested with: Subagent pressure scenarios verifying correct behavior with
global gitignore configuration. Baseline test confirmed the bug, post-fix
test confirmed correct behavior.

fix(finishing-a-development-branch): add required smoke check for runnable apps

Unit/integration tests passing does not mean a runnable app works end-to-end.
Add a REQUIRED smoke check step for web apps, servers, and CLI tools: start
the service, walk the primary user flow, run Playwright if available.

Real incident: subagent-driven-development completed 12 tasks with passing
code reviews but the app had broken CSS, missing env config, and a
non-functional UI input — none caught until the human ran it manually.

The previous wording "open in browser or use Playwright" appeared as a
bullet suggestion and was consistently skipped. This change makes the smoke
check a hard gate with explicit REQUIRED language and a clear skip condition
(docs/config-only changes only).

fix(finishing-a-development-branch): address reviewer feedback on smoke check

- Reframe skip condition around project type (no runnable process) rather
  than change type, closing the 'config-only change' rationalization that
  would have allowed skipping on broken CSS or env config
- Add explicit bridge from 'tests pass' → smoke check / Step 2
- Require agent to state the primary user flow before walking it
- Specify minimum curl/browser evidence (HTTP 200, expected content present,
  feature exercisable) instead of vague 'responds correctly'
- Clarify startup check includes warnings, not just crashes
- Normalize 'runnable apps/services' terminology

revert: restore original wording for test header and stop instruction

Two cosmetic changes from the Codex review pass were unnecessary:
- 'Run automated tests:' → 'Before presenting options, verify tests pass:'
  (original is more informative: states both action and purpose)
- 'Don't proceed until fixed.' → 'Don't proceed to Step 2.'
  (original is more precise about where not to proceed to)

Add exponential backoff and status indicator to brainstorm WebSocket client

helper.js reconnected with a fixed 1-second delay and gave no visual
feedback when disconnected. If the connection dropped (e.g., laptop
sleep, server restart), users had no indication and had to manually
refresh.

Changes:
- Exponential backoff: 500ms → 1s → 2s → ... → 30s cap, resets on success
- Status indicator: header dot updates color and text for Connected
  (green), Reconnecting (yellow), Disconnected (red)
- onerror handler prevents duplicate reconnect attempts
- Null-ify ws reference on close to avoid sends on dead socket
- frame-template.html: status dot color now uses CSS custom property
  so JavaScript can update it dynamically

Address review: remove empty onerror, show reconnecting on close

- Remove no-op onerror handler (onclose always fires after onerror)
- Change onclose status from 'disconnected' to 'reconnecting' since
  a reconnect attempt is always scheduled

Fix plan generation skipping TDD for config/tooling tasks (#853)

writing-plans mentioned TDD only in passing ("DRY. YAGNI. TDD.") without
enforcement. When early tasks involved configuration or tooling, the
planner rationalized skipping TDD and the reviewer had no criteria to
catch it.

Changes:
- Add HARD-GATE requiring RED-GREEN-REFACTOR for every code-producing task
- Add guidance for "untestable" tasks (config, build pipeline, scaffolding)
- Add anti-pattern section for "tests at the end" plans
- Add TDD Structure category to plan reviewer checklist

Address review feedback: align TDD terminology and scope

- HARD-GATE: add explicit refactor step to match RED-GREEN-REFACTOR name
- Narrow scope from "produces or modifies code" to "introduces or changes
  behavior" — refactor-only tasks rely on existing tests instead
- Reviewer prompt: align with SKILL.md wording (RED-GREEN-REFACTOR + commit)

feat: add rebase-before-merge and drift detection to finishing skill

Brainstorming now records the base branch and revision in the spec
header. Finishing reads it, pre-checks the rebase, rebases when clean,
and dispatches a drift reviewer before presenting merge options. When
rebase would conflict, conflict context is passed to the reviewer and

Refine planning and SDD skills

This commit tackles a common failure mode I tended to see while using
obra/superpowers:

1. The agent would commit full (and often incorrect) implementation
   details directly into the plan

2. Spec/code-quality reviewers would notice the error, note that the
   faulty code was copy/pasted directly from the plan, and call it ✅

3. The main agent/controller would see the passing reviews and move
   right along to the next task, leaving known-broken code intact

This is addressed with a couple of targeted adjustments to the
`writing-plans` and `subagent-driven-development` skills:

1. The planner is explicitly instructed _not_ to hard-code complete
   implementations directly into the plan, instead specifying only
   API surface areas and test names.

   This gives implementers and reviewers far more leeway to write
   (and check for) correct implementations instead of blindly
   following a bad plan. This dramatically improves code quality and
   reliability, since the implementer has access to context that
   didn't exist at planning time.

2. The main/controller agent (in `subagent-driven-development`
   workflows) is now required to evaluate the findings produced by
   reviewer subagents, and makes the final call as to whether issues
   need to be addressed before moving on.

   Since the main agent has the full "big picture" in mind, this
   avoids scope creep on the part of implementers (they're not trying
   to fix issues that would be resolved by planned follow-up tasks),
   while still ensuring that unanticipated issues are addressed when
   they're discovered.

fix: restore polyglot wrapper to fix Windows hook window spawning

Claude Code spawns hook commands with shell:true + windowsHide:true,
but on Windows the execution chain cmd.exe -> bash.exe causes Git
Bash (MSYS2) to allocate its own console window, bypassing the hide
flag. This creates visible terminal windows that steal focus on every
SessionStart event (startup, resume, clear, compact).

The fix:
- Rename session-start.sh to session-start (no extension) so Claude
  Code's .sh auto-detection regex doesn't fire and prepend "bash"
- Restore run-hook.cmd polyglot wrapper to control bash invocation
  on Windows (tries known Git Bash paths, then PATH, then exits
  silently if no bash found)
- On Unix, the polyglot's shell portion runs the script directly

This avoids Claude Code's broken .sh auto-prepend, gives us control
over how bash is invoked on Windows, and gracefully handles missing
bash instead of erroring.

extracting mechanical work to scripts. Adds a per-step decision rule,

to write a skill at all. This new section fires per step during
authoring.

fix: worktree-in-worktree uses wrong HEAD when creating new worktree

repo, causing `git worktree add -b` to branch from main's HEAD instead of
the current worktree's HEAD. Fix by capturing START_POINT before any
navigation and passing it explicitly as the start-point argument.

Codex).

feat: enhance skill descriptions for Gemini semantic auto-activation

Gemini CLI's auto-activation relies on semantic matching of the skill's
description field against the user's prompt (unlike Claude Code which
uses a rigid system instruction).

Added high-signal semantic keywords to the descriptions of core routing
skills (brainstorming, writing-plans, using-superpowers) so they natively
trigger when users ask for things like 'Let's make a react todo list'.

feat: implement deterministic skill router using BeforeAgent hook

Gemini CLI's natively built Agent Skills feature relies on probabilistic
semantic model matching for auto-activation. For Superpowers, we need
guaranteed deterministic routing (e.g., 'write a plan' must always map
to the writing-plans skill).

This commit:
- Creates agents/superpowers-router.js, a Node hook that intercepts
  prompts on the BeforeAgent lifecycle event, pattern-matches against
  Superpowers triggers, and injects strict deterministic overrides
  into the systemMessage.
- Updates .gemini/install.sh to auto-register this hook in the user's
  ~/.gemini/settings.json file.

feat: close all Gemini auto-activation gaps

Addresses 4 identified gaps in the Gemini CLI Superpowers integration:

1. Always-on gateway: when no specific regex matches, the router now
   injects a generic system message telling Gemini to check all skills
   before responding (replicates Claude's using-superpowers behavior).

2. All 14 skills now have triggers: added patterns for
   receiving-code-review, finishing-a-development-branch,
   verification-before-completion, and writing-skills. Broadened
   brainstorming triggers to catch natural language like 'Add a login
   page' (not just 'Let's build...').

3. New BeforeTool hook (superpowers-guard.js): intercepts agent tool
   calls like git commit/push/merge to inject verification and branch-
   finishing skills. These are agent-behavior triggers that can't be
   caught by user prompt matching alone.

4. Updated using-superpowers/SKILL.md with explicit Gemini CLI
   instructions for activate_skill tool alongside existing Claude Code
   instructions.

fix(finishing-a-development-branch): order branch deletion after worktree removal

Three real-world bugs caused stale `worktree-agent-*` branches to linger
after agents finished work:

1. **Wrong order in Options 1 and 4.** `git branch -d/-D` ran *before*
   `git worktree remove`, so deletion failed with "branch is checked out
   at <worktree>" — silently in some shells, leaving the branch behind.
   Branch deletion is now consolidated into Step 5, after worktree removal.

2. **No self-heal for stale registrations.** When a worktree directory
   has been deleted out-of-band (cleanup script, OS reclaim), `git worktree
   remove` errors and the registration in `.git/worktrees/` lingers
   forever, blocking future branch deletion. Added `git worktree prune -v`
   as a fallback and a defensive final prune.

3. **Buggy current-branch grep.** `git worktree list | grep $(git branch
   --show-current)` matched the *base* branch (which is current after
   Step 4's `git checkout <base>`), not the feature branch — and was
   substring-fragile besides. Replaced with an awk match on column 3 of
   `git worktree list`, anchored to `[<feature-branch>]`.

Also adds a Windows note: this skill must be invoked from the parent
context (the main repo), not from inside the worktree being cleaned up,
because Windows holds a filesystem lock on a process's own cwd.

Validated against a real cleanup of 12 stale worktree-agent branches on
a Windows host.

agent uses TDD but doesn't tell subagent to use TDD, the subagent won't
follow it. User had to repeatedly correct: "You should tell your subagents
to read the skill first, just like you did."

- Red flags for rationalizations

Base worktrees on main branch and support background setup

Fixes two critical issues with worktree creation:

1. Always base new worktrees on main/master branch by default
   - Detects repository's main branch automatically
   - Without this, worktrees inherit current branch (wrong when on feature branches)
   - Allows user override when explicitly specified

2. Document background setup optimization pattern
   - Run deps/compile in background while investigating code
   - Useful for long-running setup (large dependencies, compilation)
   - Optional pattern for when immediate worktree access not needed

Additional improvements:
- Add Elixir project support (mix deps.get && mix compile)
- Improve markdown formatting consistency

security: Fix HIGH severity vulnerabilities in brainstorm server

## Fixes Applied

### 1. WebSocket Authentication (HIGH)
- Add token-based authentication for non-localhost connections
- Generate random AUTH_TOKEN per session
- Validate token via query string or X-Auth-Token header
- Localhost connections allowed without token

### 2. Log Injection Prevention (HIGH)
- Add sanitizeForLog() function
- Remove control characters, escape newlines/tabs
- Remove path traversal patterns
- Limit string length to 1000 chars

### 3. Message Validation (MEDIUM)
- Add validateMessage() for WebSocket messages
- Block prototype pollution attempts
- Limit field count and string lengths
- Whitelist allowed fields

### 4. Rate Limiting (MEDIUM)
- Add checkRateLimit() per socket
- 100 messages per second per client
- Auto-disconnect on rate limit exceeded

### 5. Secure File Storage (MEDIUM)
- Move from /tmp to ~/.cache/superpowers/brainstorm
- Add filename validation to prevent path traversal

### 6. Memory Protection (MEDIUM)
- Add MAX_MESSAGE_SIZE limit (1MB)
- Disconnect clients exceeding limit

fix: convert all markdown files from CRLF to LF

CRLF endings can cause parsing issues when Claude Code reads

fix: remove Stop hook to avoid blocking continuation decisions

The learnings capture reminder can be handled through other means.

test: add comprehensive test infrastructure for all 16 skills

Three-tier testing framework for Superpowers skills:

Tier 1 — Skill triggering: verify correct skill activation for 16+ trigger
patterns (explicit requests, implicit context, edge cases)

Tier 2 — Pressure/behavior tests: 25+ scenarios testing skill compliance
under adversarial prompts (sunk cost, false green, YAGNI pushback, scope
creep, missing evidence, praise-only reviews, impatient users)

Tier 3 — E2E workflow chains:
  Chain A: Team lifecycle (brainstorm → plan → compose team → execute)
  Chain B: Solo lifecycle (brainstorm → plan → serial execute)
  Chain C: Cold resume (resume mid-plan from state.yml, detect plan errors)

Test helpers: run_claude wrapper, JSONL transcript parsing, assert_contains,
assert_compliance/assert_no_violation for behavioral checks,
assert_skill_invoked, assert_tool_used, project scaffolding utilities

Includes fixes for CodeRabbit findings (obra superpowers GH-664):
- A1: validate --tier arg in run-all-tests.sh
- A2: fix \| in ERE mode in chain-b run-chain.sh
- A3: verifyPassword returns false on falsy input instead of throwing

- A12: fix nested triple-backtick fence in writing-plans SKILL.md

- A14: add cleanup trap for temp dirs in test-r2 and test-r4
- A15: add missing cart to e-commerce scaffold in chain-a fixtures

feat: add .forge/ directory structure, YAML schemas, and shared knowledge templates

Creates the complete .forge/ skeleton: project.yaml, policies/default.yaml,
workflows/example.yaml, packs/, adapters/, shared/{architecture,conventions}.md,
shared/decisions/000-template.md, local/.gitignore, and bin/ directory.

Also fixes a bug in tests/forge-structure/validate-schemas.sh where yaml_has_key
was missing `import sys` in the python3 one-liner, causing all field checks to fail.

fix: address C1 command injection and C2 SQL injection in forge-state backends

C1: All python3 invocations now use `python3 - file arg...` with heredoc
source, passing file paths and user data as sys.argv — no shell interpolation
into Python source strings.

C2: SQLite backend now uses python3's sqlite3 module with parameterized
queries (?) for all DML — eliminating SQL injection from shell quoting.
Shell sqlite3 CLI fallback retained with quote-escaping for environments
without python3.

M1: All entry-point scripts changed to `set -euo pipefail`.
M2: Removed dead `_json_read_value` and `_json_unescape` helpers.
M3: All JSON writes now use temp-file + mv for atomic writes.

fix: remove hardcoded paths and stale comments in forge structure tests

- validate-schemas.sh: pass file paths via sys.argv instead of interpolating
  into Python strings/heredocs (fixes portability on other machines)
- validate-schemas.sh, validate-directory.sh: remove stale RED-phase comments
- .forge/local/.gitignore: remove redundant *.db, *.sqlite, *.json rules
  after blanket * catch-all

fix: escape LIKE wildcards in sqlite3 CLI fallback for memory query

In the no-python3 fallback path, esc_similar now escapes % and _ before
interpolation into a LIKE pattern, preventing user input from broadening
the query unexpectedly. Uses ESCAPE '\' clause to activate the escaping.

feat: add risk classification engine with policy matching and scope matrix

Implements .forge/bin/classify-risk:
- Reads all .yaml files in .forge/policies/ and matches file paths against
  glob patterns using bash native matching + python3 regex fallback for **
- Two-dimensional matrix: policy tier (blast radius) × --scope N (task count)
  → execution_strategy (solo | team-optional | team-recommended | team-required)
- Tier ordering: minimal < standard < elevated < critical; highest wins across
  multiple files and multiple matching rules
- --override <tier> forces tier, sets source=override, persists to forge-state
- Invalid tiers in policy files exit non-zero with a clear error message
- Graceful degradation when no policies directory exists (source=inferred)
- Output: key=value pairs for tier, source, execution_strategy,
  required_artifacts, matched_rules

Also fixes a bug in tests/forge-risk/test-scope-matrix.sh: the "High blast
radius" section incorrectly used auth/login.py (elevated → standard blast)
where db/migrations/001.sql (critical → high blast) was intended, causing a
contradictory expected value vs the spec example two tests later.

fix: correct artifact merge logic and add missing -e flag in classify-risk

MAJOR fixes:
- Equal-tier artifact union: when two rules match the same file at the same
  tier, artifacts are now unioned (comma-joined) instead of the second rule's
  require list being silently dropped
- Cross-file artifact tracking: capture cur_rank and new_rank BEFORE calling
  max_tier() so the comparison is between the two candidates, not between
  the updated winner and itself (which always evaluated equal, causing the
  last file's artifacts to overwrite the winner's)

MINOR fixes:
- Add -e to set flags (set -euo pipefail) for fail-fast behavior
- Replace echo -e with printf in POSIX fallback path for portability

fix: address C1 broken fallback, M1-M4 issues in enforcement hooks

C1: Replace no-op case statement in _match_policy bash fallback with a real
state machine that tracks match/tier/require per rule block and updates
best_tier/best_require when a file path matches the glob pattern.

M1: Fix single-quoted '${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}' in hooks.json that prevented
variable expansion — changed to double-quoted consistent with other entries.

M2: Add active.task forge-state fallback in forge-task-completed when
FORGE_TASK_ID env var is not set, matching the same pattern as forge-pre-commit.

M3: Use grep -F (fixed string) in _yaml_get to prevent regex injection from
key names interpolated into the grep pattern.

publishing/receiving: clean Issue bodies, adopt existing topics

Strip plugin-meta (footer, branded fallbacks, milestone "How To Pick
Up" block) from Issue/Milestone body templates so published Issues
read like normal GitHub Issues.

Receive now scans local .github-link files before claiming a new
topic number — self-publish-then-self-receive reuses the originating
topic instead of forking a parallel one with no brief or plan.

Hand-off Gate: soften preamble so refinement reads as the default

In downstream use, numbered option 1 ("Refine X") was dead text —
users always describe changes in chat, never type the digit. Preamble
now frames refinement as a natural-language default; numbered options
and HARD-GATE blocks are byte-identical, and all four gate tests pass.

issues: per-kind body shape + comments-aware receiving

Issue body now varies by Kind — bug stays structured (problem / repro /
what "fixed" looks like), enhancement and exploration are narrative —
and Out-of-Scope drops from all three (it was boilerplate; the brief
carries it for the agent picking up). receiving-issue fetches comments
alongside the body and surfaces them to writing-brief; on the adopt
path it differentiates new-vs-no-new comments by mtime so resumes can
fold in changes that arrived since the brief was last edited.

feat: batch checkbox sync and log plan-altering findings via bd comments

Two execution-skill tweaks motivated by observed agent behavior:

- executing-plans: replace per-step bd update with a single sync at task
  end. The per-step roundtrip was expensive enough that LLMs skipped it,
  defeating the recovery-on-interruption goal. Tradeoff: interrupted tasks
  re-run from the start rather than resuming mid-step. Acceptable because
  task steps are expected to be idempotent.
- executing-plans and subagent-driven-development/implementer-prompt:
  document plan-altering findings (scope shifts, approach changes, wrong
  assumptions, AC adjustments) via bd comments. Task body shows the
  current plan; comments show how we got here.

remote and nothing downstream actually depends on the push succeeding.
Git push remains the authoritative sync step.

feat(skills): add plan-quality and debug-hygiene rules; collapse brainstorming graph duplicate (1.2.19)

Derived from an audit of recent multi-model outsource reviews (GPT-5.4,
GLM-5.1, Kimi-2.6) across ~20 plan and execution reviews. Rules are phrased
generically with placeholder examples, not project-specific ones.

- writing-plans: four new sections — "Verify Before You Cite" (confirm every
  file/signature/regex in a task body against real code), "Deploy Sequence
  and Rollout Safety" (preconditions, activation gate, intermediate-state
  safety, rollback), "Cross-Layer Consistency" (single source of truth for
  multi-layer concepts or explicit drift-detection), "Semantic Regression
  Sweep for Representation Changes" (truthiness, equality, serialization,
  defaulting, formatting when a type changes shape). Plus Self-Review item
  2d — Citation Reality Check.

- systematic-debugging: Phase 1 item 6 — "Rule Out Mundane Causes Before
  Blaming Tools/Frameworks." Forces ruling out unchecked error signals,
  environment drift, permissions, language/shell semantics, stale caches,
  and concurrency before proposing a tool quirk. Prevents fabricated
  causal stories from propagating into skill edits that fix the wrong thing.

- writing-skills: new anti-pattern — "Same Instruction in Multiple Formats."
  Includes an explicit exception clause protecting deliberate multi-surface
  reinforcement (e.g. Red Flags + Rationalization table pairs) where each
  format catches a different failure mode.

- brainstorming: removed the duplicate dot graph. All branches and loops
  (visual-companion detour, design-approval loop, spec-review loop) are
  preserved in the prose Checklist and "The Process" sections.

fix(skills): bd token discipline — extract fields, don't dump records (1.2.20)

Prefer `bd show <id> --json | jq -r '.[0].<field>'` and reserve bare

refactor(brainstorming): replace shell launchers with cross-platform Node.js

Replace start-server.sh and stop-server.sh with start-server.js and
stop-server.js. Bash scripts contained platform-specific logic (nohup,
disown, SIGKILL, $PPID ps lookup, OSTYPE detection) that fails or
behaves differently on Windows. Node.js equivalents use child_process,
process.kill, and fs APIs that work consistently across platforms.

Functional parity:
- --project-dir, --host, --url-host, --foreground flags preserved
- Background detach via spawn({ detached: true }) + unref()
- Log polling loop for startup detection
- Liveness window check (catches process reaper environments)
- Graceful SIGTERM → SIGKILL escalation on Unix; TerminateProcess on Windows
- Owner PID resolution via ps on Unix; skipped on Windows (idle timeout fallback)
- Temp-only cleanup: only deletes session dir if under os.tmpdir()

markdown from companion/docs/screen-format.md, a HelpView frontend
component that fetches and renders it (with mermaid support), and a
"? Help" link in the sidebar.

feat(companion): debounce and merge demo_event stream

Coalesces consecutive demo_event POSTs with the same (screen_id, name)
within a 250ms window into a single events.jsonl line carrying the
latest data and a merged:N count. A different (screen_id, name) flushes
any pending event first in arrival order. The existing 10/sec rate-limit
stays as a safety net. Shutdown flushes all pending.

fix(companion): DecisionView fetches from the decisions repo

DecisionView was calling /api/screens/:id which does not know about
decisions, so the view hung on "Loading…". Added a new getById() on the
decisions repo returning { frontmatter, body }, a GET /api/decisions/:id
route that serves it, a getDecision client helper, and flipped
DecisionView over to the new endpoint.

fix(companion): scope width:100% away from radio/checkbox, tighten labels

The generic input, textarea { width: 100% } rule was stretching
radio and checkbox inputs to 100% of their container, which
pushed them into the middle of the form and forced the label
text onto a new line. Scope that rule to text-style inputs only
(text/email/password/number/search, textarea, select) and give
radio/checkbox explicit 14x14px dimensions with accent-color.

Introduce a shared .option class used by RadioInput, MultiInput,
and DecisionView — flex row, 8px gap, 3–4px vertical padding,
hover color change — replacing three copies of inline styles.
Labels are now visually attached to their controls and the list
is half as tall.

fix(companion): share one EventSource across all useRefresh callers

Every component that called useRefresh opened its own EventSource.
Sidebar + Activity on every page load meant two SSE sockets per tab.
With the HTTP/1.1 six-connections-per-host cap, a third tab pushed the
browser past the limit and /api/stream showed up blocked in DevTools.

A module-level singleton EventSource now fan-outs refresh events to a
Set of listeners; each useRefresh call adds and removes itself from
that Set. Latest callback captured via a ref so inline arrow functions
do not stale-close. Result: one SSE connection per tab regardless of
how many components listen.

feat(companion): replace SSE with WebSocket broadcast channel

HTTP/1.1's six-connections-per-host cap makes /api/stream show up as
blocked in DevTools once a third tab opens. WebSockets are not subject
to the same cap and Bun.serve has first-class WS support, so the push
channel moves over entirely.

- New createWsHub() exposes tryUpgrade/open/close/push/closeAll
- Bun.serve fetch intercepts GET /api/stream and upgrades via
  server.upgrade; the websocket lifecycle delegates to the hub
- RouteCtx.sse renamed to RouteCtx.broadcast with a structural type
- Frontend lib/sse.ts now manages a singleton WebSocket with auto
  reconnect; useRefresh signature is unchanged so no call sites move
- Test file renamed sse.test.ts -> ws.test.ts and rewritten against
  the new WebSocket client

fix(companion): reset view-local form state when :id changes

Preact-iso reuses a Route component instance when only the param
changes, so useState values (DecisionView note/chosen, ScreenView
values/submitted, DemoView note/html) leaked from one screen to the
next. Each view now clears its local state inside the same effect
that refetches by id.

feat(companion): decision wizard flow — parent outcomes + next-up CTA

Two related behaviors working together to make dependent decisions
feel like a wizard:

- Parent outcomes panel: a decision with depends_on now renders each
  parent as a compact card above the body, showing the parent's
  status badge, a link, the chosen_option, and the saved note. Still-
  proposed parents show a "Waiting on this decision" hint instead.
  Replaces the old "Depends on: d1, d2" plain-text line.

- Next-up CTA: after approving/revising/rejecting a decision, if any
  other decision lists this one in its depends_on and is still in
  proposed status, a big "Next: {title} ->" button renders right after
  the action row. Multiple dependents pick the first in list() order.
  No unanswered dependents renders "Done with this branch" instead.

DecisionView now fetches /api/decisions (the list) alongside the
current decision so it can compute parents and next-ups without
additional endpoints. The shared DecisionScreen schema exposes
chosen_option and note as optional fields so the list summary carries
them — previously updateStatus wrote them to the file but they were
stripped from the parsed Decision.

fix(companion): next-up wizard CTA also points at already-answered dependents

Previously the filter only considered dependents with status === "proposed",
so the button went dark as soon as a chain had been walked once. Now it
prefers the first still-proposed dependent, falls back to the first
dependent in list() order regardless of status, and labels the button
"Next: {title}" vs "Continue to: {title}" based on that parent's state.
A true leaf (no dependents at all) still shows "Done with this branch".

fix(companion): dedupe /api/decisions/:id on identical status/chosen/note

Accidental double-clicks on Approve used to append two identical
decision events to events.jsonl and rewrite the file twice. The route
now compares the incoming {status, chosen_option, note} tuple against
the current file; a no-op submission returns {ok: true, duplicate: true}
without touching decisions-repo or events-writer. A real change (even
just a different note with the same status+chosen) still logs as before.

feat(companion): --reset flag wipes session state on start for dev loops

companion start --session-dir X --reset now clears screens/,
decisions/, events.jsonl, and any rotated events-*.jsonl logs before
ensureSessionDir runs. Meant for dev iteration: wire it into whatever
rebuild script you use and each restart comes up empty.

resetSessionDir() is a no-op on an empty or missing directory so the
flag is safe to leave in a dev command permanently.

fix(companion): dedupe treats omitted body fields as "keep existing"

The previous dedupe compared (current ?? undefined) against (body ??
undefined), so a submission that omitted note got treated as "note was
cleared", which never matches a file that still holds a note — the
guard fired, the file kept its old note, but the event log claimed the
note was gone. The update path itself already uses keep-existing
semantics for undefined fields, so the dedupe now mirrors that: an
undefined chosen_option or note in the body means "do not compare this
dimension," and a real value still has to match.

fix(companion): dark-theme CodeMirror + tinted file-edit card

CodeMirror's default highlight style is light-on-white, so the editor
blended into the shell background and the gutter rendered a bright
chip that fought the dark palette. .cm-host now sets its own #0b0d12
background and overrides .cm-editor / .cm-scroller / .cm-gutters /
.cm-activeLine classes to match the rest of the companion's theme,
with a subtle blue accent on the caret, active line, and selection.

Also gave .file-edit a faint yellow tint so the dashed-outline
privacy card layers visually above the page instead of sitting flat.

feat(companion): graceful shutdown flow with shutting-down / ended screens

Adds an LLM-initiated end-of-session ceremony:

Server
- Bun.serve shutdown() now broadcasts {event: "shutdown", data: {reason}}
  over the WebSocket, delays 1500ms so connected clients can paint the
  shutting-down screen, then runs the existing teardown (flush demo,
  close sockets, write server-stopped, stop).
- New POST /api/shutdown route takes {reason?} and schedules a call to
  ctx.shutdown after the response flushes. The shutdown callback is
  plumbed through RouteCtx instead of raw process.kill so tests can
  observe the flow without killing their own runner.
- Shutdown is idempotent via a shuttingDown guard; SIGTERM/SIGINT,
  stopRunning, and /api/shutdown all funnel through the same path.

Client
- lib/sse.ts listens for {event: "shutdown"}, persists a ShutdownState
  ({phase: "shutting_down" | "ended", reason}) to sessionStorage so
  the state survives soft refreshes, and suppresses WebSocket
  reconnect once shutdown has started.
- New useShutdownState() hook exposes the state to components.
- App now short-circuits to a new EndedView when phase !== "live",
  so any route goes through the ceremony regardless of where the
  user was when shutdown fired.
- EndedView shows "Shutting down…" with a spinner while WS is still
  open, then "Session ended — you can close this window" after the
  socket closes. Returning to the tab later re-renders the ended
  state straight from sessionStorage.

Tests
- packages/server/test/shutdown.test.ts covers the WebSocket-frame
  and POST /api/shutdown paths end-to-end against a real Bun server.

experiment(skills): apply wenyan-full to verification-before-completion

Pilot of wenyan-full compression on an agent-internal skill with no user
interaction. Preserves frontmatter description (trigger matching), all
code blocks, rule strings in English, table column headers, and the
"human partner" tuned phrase. Prose body and table cells translated to
classical Chinese.

experiment(eval): A/B harness for wenyan skill pilot

Small shell harness using the glm alias (claude CLI + z.ai backend) to
feed skill-then-scenario prompts and capture text responses. One pilot
run committed for verification-before-completion: both English control
and wenyan-full variants correctly refused a premature completion claim
and demanded verification. Wenyan version surfaced more Gate Function
detail (exit code, failure count). Single trial — not conclusive.

experiment(skills+eval): wenyan-full systematic-debugging + A/B scenarios 2 & 3

- skills/systematic-debugging/SKILL.md: wenyan-full conversion. Preserves
  frontmatter description, code blocks, phase headings, "human partner"
  tuned phrase, Iron Law rule string.
- eval/wenyan/scenario-2.md: adversarial "linter passed, ship it?" test.
  Both control and wenyan correctly block rationalization.
- eval/wenyan/scenario-debug.md: flaky integration test with 2 prior
  failed patches. Wenyan output invokes Phase 1 + Phase 3 structure
  explicitly; control misses phase labels. Wenyan 9/9 on rubric vs 7/9
  control.
- eval/wenyan/README.md: updated with n=3 summary.

Signal across 3 scenarios: no behavioral degradation; wenyan tracks
phase structure more explicitly in the debugging scenario. Still single
backend (glm-5-turbo via z.ai), small n.

experiment(skills+eval): full wenyan-full conversion + A/B eval (5 skills)

Skills converted to wenyan-full on this branch:
- verification-before-completion
- systematic-debugging
- subagent-driven-development
- test-driven-development
- dispatching-parallel-agents

All preserve: frontmatter descriptions (trigger matching), fenced code
and dot-graph blocks, iron-law rule strings, "human partner" tuned
phrases, illustrative workflow code examples, and table column headers.

Eval harness (eval/wenyan/) parameterized over backend aliases in
~/.bashrc. 16 runs across 2 backends (glm via z.ai, minmax via
minimax.io) and 6 scenarios targeting each skill's rationalization
surfaces. Zero behavioral degradation observed. Wenyan occasionally
foregrounded the skeletal phase/rule structure more explicitly than
the English control; scenario 3 (systematic-debugging) scored 9/9 vs
control 7/9 on the rubric.

Cross-model check on scenarios 2 and 6 held on minmax. Limitations,
scope caveats, and reproduction steps in eval/wenyan/README.md.

Not intended for upstream merge. Superpowers project CLAUDE.md
explicitly gates skill-content changes on adversarial eval evidence;
this pilot is the start of that evidence, not the end.

experiment(eval): writing-skills A/B on Sonnet — no degradation

User hypothesized writing-skills might degrade under wenyan-full.
Tested with Sonnet (default Anthropic backend, not glm) on a scenario
that combined three rationalization surfaces: skip-baseline-testing,
description-summarizes-workflow, and "it's too simple."

Both control and wenyan correctly refused the request. Both cited the
Iron Law, the CSO anti-pattern, and the rationalization table. Wenyan
quoted the rationalization table verbatim ("Too simple to test → Simple
code breaks. Test takes 30 seconds.") and additionally caught a
security issue (unconditional rm -rf as footgun) that the control
response did not flag.

Rubric: control 6/7, wenyan 7/7 + 1 bonus security catch.

Also extended run.sh to support 'claude' as alias-name, which uses the
stock claude CLI (no env override, defaults to user's Anthropic config).

fix(opencode): cache bootstrap content at module level to eliminate per-step file I/O

getBootstrapContent() called fs.existsSync + fs.readFileSync + regex
frontmatter parsing on every agent step with zero caching.  The
experimental.chat.messages.transform hook fires every step in opencode's
agent loop (messages are reloaded from DB each step via
filterCompactedEffect).  A 10-step turn triggered 10 redundant file
reads + 10 regex parses for content that never changes during a session.

Changes:
- Add module-level _bootstrapCache (undefined = not loaded, null = file
  missing) so the first call reads and parses SKILL.md, all subsequent
  calls return the cached string with zero filesystem access
- Cache the null sentinel when SKILL.md is missing, preventing repeated
  fs.existsSync probes
- Add _testing export (resetCache/getCache) for test infrastructure
- Clarify the injection guard comment explaining how it interacts with
  opencode's per-step message reloading
- Add 15 regression tests covering cache behavior, fs call counts,
  injection guard, missing file sentinel, cache reset, and source audit

Fixes #1202

fix(react-view-transitions): plain-prose description for upload validator

Closes #214.

Claude's skill upload validator rejects SKILL.md when the description
frontmatter field contains XML tags ("SKILL.md description cannot
contain XML tags"). The description had backtick-wrapped XML-style
tokens like \`<ViewTransition>\` that the validator treats as XML.

Rewrites the offending tokens in plain prose ("the `ViewTransition`
component" instead of "`<ViewTransition>` component"), preserving the
same intent and trigger phrases. Other places in the SKILL.md that
already used the bare component name (`ViewTransition`) are unchanged.

Repackaged skills/react-view-transitions.zip from the updated source.
Swept all other skills/*/SKILL.md descriptions; none had the same
issue.
fix(react-native-skills): add platform notes for iOS-only ScrollView props

feat(rules): push use client to leaves, pass RSCs as children

Two new server-side rules covering the most common mistakes at the
server/client boundary: unnecessary use-client directives that inflate
the JS bundle, and direct imports of Server Components inside Client
Components that silently force them client-side.

Translate 32 previously untranslated documentation files covering:

All files follow the established .zh-CN.md naming convention.

docs(vercel-cli-with-tokens): resolve VERCEL_ORG_ID from team slug

Some integrations (notably Stripe Projects / Fabric) provision
VERCEL_TOKEN and VERCEL_PROJECT_ID but do not emit VERCEL_ORG_ID.
The existing pair requirement (both ORG_ID + PROJECT_ID, or neither)
blocks the CLI from running with only a project ID. Add a step
showing how to resolve the org ID from a team slug via
`vercel teams ls --format json`.
